# System design document for DesFlow (2026-02-12 12:05Z)

_**User (2026-02-12 12:14Z)**_

📘 System Design Document
Project Name: DesFlow(Working Title)
Version: 1.0 (MVP)
Author: Shruti Gitte
Date: 2026
1. Introduction
1.1 Background
Modern software teams use multiple disconnected tools for product development:
Figma for UI/UX design
Jira for task tracking
Slack for communication
This fragmented workflow leads to inefficiencies, miscommunication, and loss of context.
1.2 Problem Statement
There is no lightweight, affordable platform that seamlessly integrates:
Design context
Task management
Team communication
into a single unified workflow.
1.3 Proposed Solution
DesFlow is a SaaS platform that integrates Figma designs with a Jira-like task management system and built-in chat. It allows teams to create development issues directly from design elements and collaborate within the same environment.
2. Objectives
Reduce context switching between tools
Improve design-to-development handoff
Centralize project communication
Support small and mid-sized teams
Provide an extensible platform
3. Scope
3.1 In Scope (MVP)
User authentication
Project and team management
Figma plugin integration
Issue tracking system
Embedded design preview
Kanban board
Issue and project chat
Role-based access control
3.2 Out of Scope (MVP)
Full design editor
Mobile apps
Offline support
AI automation
Enterprise compliance
4. System Architecture
4.1 High-Level Architecture
+-------------+       +------------------+
| Figma Plugin| <---> | Backend API       |
+-------------+       +------------------+
                             |
                             v
+-------------+       +------------------+
| Web Client  | <---> | PostgreSQL DB     |
+-------------+       +------------------+
        |
        v
+----------------+
| WebSocket Hub  |
+----------------+
4.2 Components
Component	Description
Figma Plugin	Captures design context
Web Client	User interface
Backend API	Business logic
Database	Data storage
WebSocket Hub	Real-time communication
5. Technology Stack
Frontend
Next.js
TypeScript
TailwindCSS
Backend
Node.js (NestJS / API Routes)
Prisma ORM
Database
PostgreSQL
Realtime
Socket.IO
Authentication
NextAuth.js
Cloud
Vercel, Supabase/Neon, AWS S3
6. Functional Requirements
6.1 Authentication & Authorization
Email/password login
Google OAuth
JWT-based sessions
RBAC with roles:
Owner
Admin
Member
Viewer
6.2 Project Management
Create and manage projects
Invite members
Role assignment
Permission enforcement
6.3 Issue Management
Each issue contains:
Field	Description
Title	Issue summary
Description	Detailed info
Status	Todo/InProgress/Review/Done
Priority	Low/Medium/High
Assignee	Responsible user
Figma File Key	Linked file
Node ID	Design node
Thumbnail	Design preview
Created At	Timestamp
Features:
CRUD
Filtering
Sprint assignment
6.4 Figma Integration
Plugin Capabilities
Read selected node
Export preview image
Capture metadata
Authenticate user
Call backend APIs
Workflow
Select element in Figma
Click “Create Issue”
Plugin sends metadata
Backend creates issue
6.5 Design Preview
Display thumbnail
Embed Figma viewer (optional)
Deep link to node
6.6 Kanban Board
Drag and drop issues
Status synchronization
Sprint management
6.7 Chat System
Types
Issue-level chat
Project-level chat
Features
Real-time messaging
Mentions
File upload (future)
6.8 Activity Logging
Issue creation/updates
Status changes
Membership changes
7. Non-Functional Requirements
7.1 Performance
API latency < 300 ms
Chat latency < 100 ms
7.2 Security
HTTPS
Encrypted tokens
Input validation
RBAC checks
7.3 Scalability
Stateless backend
Horizontal scaling
Redis (future)
7.4 Reliability
Automated backups
Graceful reconnect
8. Database Design
User
id, email, name, createdAt
Project
id, name, ownerId, createdAt
Membership
userId, projectId, role
Issue
id, projectId, title, status, priority,
assigneeId, figmaFileKey, nodeId,
thumbnailUrl, createdAt
Message
id, projectId, issueId?, senderId,
content, createdAt
ActivityLog
id, actorId, entityType, entityId,
action, payload, timestamp
9. API Design (Examples)
Create Issue
POST /api/issues
Get Board
GET /api/projects/{id}/board
Send Chat
POST /api/messages
WebSocket Events
issue:created
issue:updated
chat:message
user:online
10. Deployment
Infrastructure
Frontend: Vercel
Backend: Render/Fly.io
Database: Supabase/Neon
Storage: AWS S3 / Cloudflare R2
CI/CD
GitHub Actions
11. Development Roadmap
Phase	Duration	Goals
Phase 1	2 weeks	Auth, Projects, Issues
Phase 2	2 weeks	Figma Plugin, Preview
Phase 3	2 weeks	Chat, Realtime
Phase 4	1 week	Polish
12. Risks & Mitigation
Risk	Mitigation
API limits	Caching
Plugin changes	Versioning
Scaling	Redis
Data loss	Backups
13. Future Enhancements
Version history
AI summaries
GitHub integration
Automation rules
Analytics
14. Success Metrics
Issue creation time < 10s
User retention
Task completion rate
Chat engagement
15. Conclusion
DesignFlow provides a unified platform for integrating design, development, and collaboration. By embedding Figma context into project management workflows, it significantly improves productivity and communication.

---

### Summary of DesFlow MVP Design

You’ve got a very solid, well-scoped MVP: clear problem, target users (small/mid teams), and a realistic feature set that avoids “platform bloat” (no full editor, no mobile, no AI yet). The stack and architecture are also coherent for a modern SaaS (Next.js + TS + Prisma + Postgres + Socket.IO).

### Strengths

- **Clear problem framing**: Context switching between Figma / Jira / Slack is explicit and relatable.
- **Focused scope**: Auth, projects, issues, Figma link, Kanban, and chat are the right “thin slice” to validate value.
- **Good domain model**: `User`, `Project`, `Membership`, `Issue`, `Message`, `ActivityLog` is clean and extensible.
- **Reasonable NFRs**: Latency, RBAC, scalability assumptions (stateless, WebSocket hub) and future Redis are realistic for SaaS.

### Gaps / Refinements to Consider

- **Multi-tenancy & org model**  
  - You implicitly treat `Project` as the top-level container. Decide if you need `Organization`/`Workspace` for future pricing tiers and isolation.  
  - Clarify whether a `User` can belong to multiple “orgs” and how billing will eventually work.

- **Auth model detail**  
  - You list Email/Password + Google OAuth + NextAuth + JWT. Decide: NextAuth with database sessions only, or JWT-based access tokens for the Figma plugin and Web client?  
  - Document token lifetimes, refresh strategy, and how the plugin authenticates (e.g., personal access token created via web app and stored in plugin).

- **RBAC rules**  
  - Explicitly specify which role can: create/edit/delete projects, manage members, delete issues/messages, configure Figma integration, etc.  
  - Decide whether `Owner` is per-project or per-organization, and how transfer of ownership works.

- **Figma plugin interaction contract**  
  - Define the exact APIs the plugin calls (`POST /api/plugin/issues`, `POST /api/plugin/auth/exchange-token`, etc.), their payload shapes, and expected responses.  
  - Clarify how you map Figma users ↔ DesFlow users (email matching, token handoff, or manual link).

- **Realtime architecture**  
  - Decide if WebSockets are served from the same backend as REST or a separate “WebSocket Hub” service.  
  - Define channel/room structure (e.g., `project:{id}`, `issue:{id}`, `user:{id}`) and which events go where.  
  - Plan for presence (`user:online`) – store in-memory vs Redis later.

- **Kanban + sprints model**  
  - You mention “Sprint assignment” but no explicit `Sprint` entity. Decide:  
    - Minimal: `Issue` has `sprintId` (nullable) and you add `Sprint(id, projectId, name, startDate, endDate)`.  
    - Or drop sprints from MVP and add them in Phase 2/3.

- **ActivityLog normalization**  
  - `entityType` should be an enum (`'issue' | 'project' | 'membership' | ...`) and `action` should also be constrained (`'created' | 'updated' | 'status_changed' | ...`).  
  - Decide if `payload` will be a JSON diff of before/after or just “after” state.

- **File storage for thumbnails**  
  - Clarify where thumbnails are generated (plugin vs backend) and upload flow (plugin → backend signed URL → S3/R2).  
  - Add constraints: max image size, format (PNG/JPEG), and how you handle regeneration when design changes.

- **Validation & error-handling strategy**  
  - Note use of a schema validator (e.g., Zod) for both API routes and client-side forms.  
  - Add a standard error envelope (e.g., `{ error: { code, message, details } }`).

### Suggested Next Artifacts

If you’d like, I can help you create any/all of:

- **Database schema (Prisma)** for MVP, with enums and relations.
- **Initial API spec** (OpenAPI-style or detailed route list with request/response bodies).
- **Sequence diagrams** for key flows:
  - Figma “Create Issue from Node”
  - Issue status change (Kanban drag-drop)
  - Issue-level chat with WebSocket events
- **Next.js project structure** (pages/app routes, modules for auth, projects, issues, chat).

Tell me which of these you want to work on next (e.g. “Let’s start with Prisma schema” or “Help me design the plugin-backend API”), and I’ll draft it concretely.

_**User (2026-02-12 12:18Z)**_

📘 DesignFlow MVP Design Document (v1.1)
0. TL;DR
DesignFlow is a Jira-lite web app integrated with Figma via a plugin to create issues from selected nodes, show design previews inside the issue, provide deep links back to Figma, and enable issue/project chat via WebSockets.
1. Goals
1.1 Goals (MVP)
Create issues from Figma selection (nodeId + fileKey)
View issue in web app with thumbnail + “Open in Figma”
Kanban board with drag-drop status updates
Issue chat + Project chat (real-time)
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) at project level
Activity log for major issue actions
1.2 Non-Goals (MVP)
Full design editor (no Figma clone)
DM chat
Advanced JQL search
Automation rules
Enterprise SSO (Single Sign-On)
Full sprint analytics (burndown, velocity)
2. Users & Use Cases
2.1 Target users
Small / mid product teams
Freelancers + clients
Student teams / hackathons
2.2 Key MVP use cases
Designer selects a Figma node → creates issue
Developer opens issue in web app → sees preview + opens Figma at exact node
Team discusses on issue chat thread
PM drags issue across Kanban → status updates for all viewers
Activity log shows “who changed what”
3. Multi-tenancy model (Org/Workspace decision)
3.1 Decision
Add Organization (Workspace) now (minimal) to avoid refactor later for billing/isolation.
Hierarchy
User → Organization → Project → Issue
3.2 Membership
Users can belong to multiple organizations.
3.3 Billing (future)
Billing attaches to Organization, not project.
4. Authentication & Token Model
4.1 Web app auth
Use NextAuth (Google OAuth + Credentials optional)
Sessions stored in DB (recommended) for simplicity
4.2 Plugin auth (important)
Figma plugin cannot rely on NextAuth cookie sessions reliably.
Use a Personal Access Token (PAT) created from the web app:
User logs into web app
Generates a PAT tied to user + org
Copies token into Figma plugin “Connect”
Plugin stores token locally (Figma client storage)
Token properties:
Hashed in DB
Scope-limited: plugin:write_issues, plugin:read_projects
Revocable
Expiry: 30–90 days (configurable)
Reason: simplest secure flow for MVP, avoids OAuth complexity inside plugin.
5. Authorization (RBAC)
5.1 Roles
Org roles: ORG_OWNER, ORG_ADMIN, ORG_MEMBER
Project roles: PROJECT_ADMIN, PROJECT_MEMBER, PROJECT_VIEWER
5.2 Permission Matrix (MVP)
Org
ORG_OWNER: manage org, billing (future), delete org, manage members
ORG_ADMIN: manage members, create projects
ORG_MEMBER: access assigned projects
Project
PROJECT_ADMIN:
create/edit/delete issues
change issue status
manage project members/roles
configure Figma integration
PROJECT_MEMBER:
create/edit issues
change status
chat
PROJECT_VIEWER:
view issues + design previews
read chat only (or allow chat read/write; choose one—MVP: read-only)
Ownership transfer (future): ORG_OWNER can transfer ownership.
6. Figma Integration
6.1 What is stored per issue
figmaFileKey
nodeId
figmaNodeName (optional)
thumbnailUrl (generated from plugin)
figmaDeepLinkUrl (computed from fileKey/nodeId)
6.2 Plugin → Backend contract (MVP endpoints)
All plugin endpoints require Authorization: Bearer <PAT>.
6.2.1 List projects user can use
GET /api/plugin/projects?orgId=...
Response: projects with permissions + allowed actions.
6.2.2 Create issue from selection
POST /api/plugin/issues
Request:
{
  "projectId": "proj_123",
  "title": "Implement Login Button",
  "description": "From Figma selection",
  "priority": "HIGH",
  "figmaFileKey": "AbCdEf",
  "nodeId": "45:89",
  "nodeName": "Login Button",
  "thumbnailUpload": {
    "contentType": "image/png",
    "sizeBytes": 345678
  }
}
Response:
{
  "issueId": "iss_123",
  "upload": {
    "url": "https://signed-upload-url",
    "method": "PUT",
    "headers": { "Content-Type": "image/png" }
  }
}
Then plugin uploads thumbnail to storage, and confirms:
POST /api/plugin/issues/{issueId}/thumbnail/complete
{ "thumbnailUrl": "https://cdn.../iss_123.png" }
This “signed URL upload” flow avoids pushing large images through your server.
7. Web App UX (Core Screens)
7.1 Project Dashboard
Project list + members
Link to board + project chat
7.2 Kanban Board
Columns: Todo / In Progress / Review / Done
Drag drop issue → updates status
Realtime update to other clients
7.3 Issue Detail Page
Left:
title, description, status, priority, assignee
thumbnail preview
button: Open in Figma
Right:
Issue Chat thread
Activity log (small)
7.4 Project Chat
One channel per project (“General”)
8. Realtime Architecture (WebSockets)
8.1 Deployment choice (MVP)
Serve WebSocket from the same backend initially (simpler).
Later split into “WebSocket Hub” if needed.
8.2 Rooms / Channels
org:{orgId} (optional)
project:{projectId}
issue:{issueId}
user:{userId} (notifications later)
8.3 Socket Events
Issues
issue:created → project room
issue:updated → project + issue room
issue:status_changed → project room
Chat
chat:project_message → project room
chat:issue_message → issue room
Presence (optional MVP+)
presence:join, presence:leave in project rooms
Storage: in-memory now, Redis later.
9. Kanban + Sprints Decision
9.1 MVP decision
Kanban only in MVP.
Sprints introduce additional entities + analytics complexity.
9.2 Phase 2 (Sprint entity)
Add:
Sprint(id, projectId, name, startDate, endDate, status)
Issue.sprintId nullable
10. Activity Log (Audit-lite)
10.1 Data shape
entityType enum: ISSUE, PROJECT, MEMBERSHIP
action enum: CREATED, UPDATED, STATUS_CHANGED, ASSIGNED, COMMENTED
payload JSON: store minimal “diff” or just new values
MVP: store “after” fields for simplicity.
Example payload:
{
  "field": "status",
  "from": "TODO",
  "to": "IN_PROGRESS"
}
11. Storage for Thumbnails
11.1 Generation
Generated by Figma plugin using export PNG from selected node/frame.
11.2 Upload
Plugin requests signed URL → uploads to S3/R2 → confirms completion.
11.3 Constraints
Max size: 2–5 MB
Formats: PNG (preferred), JPEG allowed
Thumbnail regeneration: manual “Refresh from Figma” later
12. Validation + Error Handling
12.1 Validation
Use Zod for:
API request bodies
Client-side forms
Plugin request bodies
12.2 Error envelope (standard)
All errors return:
{
  "error": {
    "code": "FORBIDDEN",
    "message": "You do not have permission",
    "details": {}
  }
}
Error codes:
UNAUTHORIZED
FORBIDDEN
NOT_FOUND
VALIDATION_ERROR
RATE_LIMITED
INTERNAL_ERROR
13. Database Model (High Level)
Entities:
User
Organization
OrgMembership
Project
ProjectMembership
Issue
Message
ActivityLog
PersonalAccessToken
Attachment (optional later)
Key indexes:
Membership uniqueness
(userId, orgId) unique
(userId, projectId) unique
Issue queries
(projectId, status)
(assigneeId, status)
Messages
(issueId, createdAt)
(projectId, createdAt)
14. Security Considerations
PAT stored hashed, never plaintext
Signed URL uploads limited to correct mime + max size
RBAC enforced on every read/write route
Rate limiting on plugin endpoints
CORS locked down
15. Roadmap
Phase 1 (MVP)
Org + projects
Issues + Kanban
Figma plugin create issue + thumbnail
Issue & project chat (Socket)
Activity log basic
Phase 2
Sprints
Mention notifications
Better search
Presence improvements + Redis
Phase 3
Billing (Stripe) per organization
Automation rules
GitHub integration
